name: Singapore
tagline: Where strict rules meet incredible food

scenarios.description: Immigration, customs, and arrival at Changi

arrivalSteps.title: SG Arrival Card (SGAC) Submission
arrivalSteps.description: All visitors must submit the SG Arrival Card with health declaration via the ICA website (ica.gov.sg/arrive) or the MyICA mobile app within 3 days before arrival. This is mandatory and replaces the paper disembarkation card. Failure to submit is a legal offence.

arrivalSteps.title: Passport Control / Automated Clearance
arrivalSteps.description: Many nationalities from visa-exempt countries can use Automated Immigration Lanes (e-Gates) with a biometric passport. First-time visitors may need to enroll fingerprints at a manual counter. Present your passport and SGAC confirmation QR code.

arrivalSteps.title: Biometric Enrollment (first-time visitors)
arrivalSteps.description: First-time visitors at manual counters will have fingerprints and a facial photograph captured. Returning visitors with stored biometrics can use Automated Immigration Lanes for faster clearance.

arrivalSteps.title: Baggage Claim
arrivalSteps.description: Collect luggage from the assigned carousel in the baggage hall. Random baggage screening is conducted by Singapore Customs X-ray before the exit.

arrivalSteps.title: Customs Declaration
arrivalSteps.description: GST relief applies on overseas goods up to SGD 500. Duty-free alcohol: 2L wine/beer and 1L spirits (for stays over 48 hours outside Singapore). There is NO duty-free tobacco allowance — all tobacco must be declared and duty paid. Prohibited: chewing gum (therapeutic types excepted), e-cigarettes, firecrackers.

arrivalSteps.title: Prohibited Goods — Serious Offences
arrivalSteps.description: Singapore enforces strict penalties including mandatory death penalty for drug trafficking above threshold quantities. Carry only prescribed medications with a doctor's letter. Declare all firearms, ammunition, and controlled items at the Red Channel.

fallbackAlerts.message: Chewing gum is banned in Singapore. Do not bring it in your luggage — confiscation and fines apply.
All tobacco products must be declared at customs. There is no duty-free tobacco allowance in Singapore.

scenarios.description: Food court etiquette, chope culture, and ordering

arrivalSteps.title: Chope Your Seat First
arrivalSteps.description: Leave a tissue packet or umbrella on an empty seat to reserve it. This is widely practised and respected.

arrivalSteps.title: Return Your Tray
arrivalSteps.description: Since 2021 it is mandatory to return your tray and clear your table after eating. Fines apply.

arrivalSteps.title: Order Directly at Stalls
arrivalSteps.description: Walk to each stall to order and collect your food. Payment is usually at point of order.

fallbackAlerts.message: Returning your tray at hawker centres is mandatory by law since 2021. Fines can be issued.

fallbackAlerts.description: A tissue packet on a seat means it is reserved — this is the local 'chope' system. Respect it.
fallbackAlerts.message: A tissue packet on a seat means it is reserved — this is the local 'chope' system. Respect it.

phrases.english: One plate of chicken rice, please.

scenarios.description: MRT rules, fines, and transit etiquette

arrivalSteps.title: No Food or Drink
arrivalSteps.description: Eating and drinking on the MRT or buses is strictly prohibited. Fines up to SGD 500.

arrivalSteps.title: Priority Seats
arrivalSteps.description: Priority seats are for the elderly, pregnant, and disabled. Vacate them if needed — passengers will ask.

arrivalSteps.title: Queue to Board
arrivalSteps.description: Form an orderly queue along the marked lines on the platform. Board only after passengers have exited.

fallbackAlerts.message: No eating or drinking on the MRT or buses. Fines of up to SGD 500 apply.
Durian (the fruit) is banned on all public transport in Singapore due to its odour.

phrases.english: Which line goes to Orchard Road?

phrases.romanized: Which line goes to Orchard Road?

scenarios.description: Multi-faith etiquette at Singapore's temples, mosques, and churches

arrivalSteps.title: Remove Shoes at Temples
arrivalSteps.description: Remove footwear before entering Hindu and Buddhist temples. Mosques also require bare feet or socks.

arrivalSteps.title: Modest Dress
arrivalSteps.description: Cover shoulders and knees at all places of worship. Sarongs are available to borrow at some entrances.

fallbackAlerts.message: Remove your shoes before entering Hindu temples and mosques. Racks are provided at the entrance.
